content.text: Hook Call Order and State Identity

Every hook call in a React component is tracked by its position in the call sequence — not by a name, a key, or any identifier you provide. React assigns slot 0 to the first hook, slot 1 to the second, and so on. On the next render, it walks the same sequence and restores each slot's stored value. The term
refers to this slot-based association: a hook's identity
its call-order position in a given component.

attrs.alt: Hook call order diagram showing fiber slot assignment across renders

content.text: Each React fiber node (the internal object representing one component instance) carries a
property that points to the head of a singly linked list of hook objects. Every hook call during render appends one object to this list or walks to the next existing object. React tracks a global
cursor that advances by one step on each hook call —
. If a hook call is skipped, the cursor never advances for that slot, and all subsequent hooks read from the slot one position too early.
Each hook object in the linked list has the same shape, regardless of whether the hook is
Mount vs Update: How the Cursor Works
React distinguishes two phases for hook execution. During
(the first render), each hook call appends a new hook object to the fiber's linked list and initializes it with the hook's initial value or setup function. During

(re-render), each hook call reads from the next existing hook object rather than creating one. The cursor starts at the head of the list and advances on each call. The correctness of the update phase depends entirely on the list being identical in length and order to what was built during mount.
What Happens When a Hook is Skipped

When a hook is conditionally skipped, the linked list has the same length as before (the old hook objects still exist), but the cursor walks them in a different mapping. A component that previously had

in slot 0 now skips it, so the second hook reads slot 0 instead of slot 1. The data in slot 0 is whatever was stored there before — not the data the second hook expects. The result ranges from reading stale state to reading an object of the wrong type, which often causes a runtime error.

React detects slot-count mismatches during the update phase: if the cursor reaches the end of the existing linked list before all hook calls are processed, or finishes hook calls before the list is exhausted, it throws
Rendered more hooks than during the previous render
or the inverse. This error is thrown eagerly — React does not silently read wrong data and continue.

, not per component type. When a component unmounts, its fiber node (and the linked list of hook objects) is discarded. If the same component type remounts, React allocates a fresh fiber with a fresh linked list, re-running all initializers. This is why unmounting and remounting a component resets all its state — it is a new identity, even for the same type at the same DOM position, unless a
or structural trick forces React to reuse the fiber.
Keys as an Identity Reset Mechanism

prop directly manipulates state identity. Two elements of the same type at the same DOM position normally share their fiber across renders. Adding different keys — or changing a key — tells React to destroy the old fiber and create a new one. This is the mechanism behind intentionally resetting a component's state: give it a new
and React discards the old linked list.
Multiple useState Calls and Slot Ordering
call independently maps to one slot. They do not share a slot or merge their values. The order of calls determines which slot each one gets. This is why swapping the order of two
calls is a breaking change — slot 0's stored value becomes slot 1's reading, and vice versa — even though no state was explicitly changed.
useRef, useMemo, and useCallback in the Linked List
also occupies a slot in the same linked list. Its hook object stores
at initialization. On subsequent renders, the cursor advances past the
slot without re-running any initialization. Because
does not cause re-renders when mutated, its slot is stable and the mutable
property persists across renders — but it still requires a stable call position.

in their slot. On re-render, React reads the stored deps array and shallowly compares it with the new deps. If they match, the stored value is returned without re-running the factory. If they differ, the factory reruns and the new
pair is written back to the same slot. The slot identity ensures the comparison is always between the previous and current render's deps for the same hook call.
Custom Hooks Expand Into the Same List

Custom hooks participate in the same linked list as built-in hooks. A custom hook is just a function that calls built-in hooks — those calls expand inline into the calling component's slot sequence. If
, those two calls occupy two consecutive slots in the component's list. Calling
conditionally therefore violates rule 1 for both slots it occupies.
In concurrent mode, React may render a component multiple times before committing to the DOM (speculative rendering for features like

). Each speculative render walks the same linked list in the same order. A stable hook order is even more important in concurrent mode because React may abandon a speculative render and restart it — the restart must produce identical slot assignments to avoid state corruption.
Reading Hook State in React DevTools

React DevTools exposes the linked list to developers. The Components panel shows each hook in call order, labeled by hook type, with its current value. Hooks from custom hooks are shown as a tree — the custom hook name is the parent, its internal hooks are children. This tree is a direct rendering of the linked list structure. If a hook appears in the wrong position in DevTools across renders, a rule violation is the likely cause.
When a hook order bug occurs, the React error message names the fiber component but not which specific hook moved. A systematic approach makes it faster to find the violation:

content: : React names the component that violated the rules. The message also says whether more or fewer hooks were rendered — this tells you if a hook was added or removed.

# Find the React version tree — multiple entries mean duplicate copies
npm ls react

# Or with pnpm:
pnpm why react
Reading the linked list directly from the fiber is not a stable API and changes between React versions. Use React DevTools or add targeted
calls around suspected hook calls during debugging.
useContext and useReducer in the Linked List

also occupies a slot. Its hook object stores the current context value at render time. On re-render, React checks if the context value changed; if so, it queues a re-render of the subscribing component. The slot must remain stable just like any other hook — calling
inside a condition leads to the same slot-shift problem as any other hook.
is implemented on top of. Its slot stores the same
holds the full reducer state object and the queue contains dispatched actions. When you call
function is stable across renders (same reference) because it is derived from the slot's queue object, not the state value.
Quick reference for which hooks affect slot count and how their stored value changes across renders:
Returns stored value; processes pending setState calls
Runs reducer over queued actions; returns new state
Compares deps; schedules fn after paint if changed
Same as useEffect but fires synchronously after DOM mutation
Returns same object reference every render; no re-render on mutation
Returns stored value if deps unchanged; reruns fn otherwise
Returns stored fn if deps unchanged; stores new fn otherwise
Subscribes to context; re-renders if context value changes
performs static analysis of hook call sites. It understands function naming (
prefix), JSX context, and call-site depth. It catches common violations at the editor level before runtime:

# Violations the linter catches:
# 1. Hook inside an if/else block
# 2. Hook inside a for/while loop
# 3. Hook inside a non-hook, non-component function
# 4. Hook after an early return
# 5. Conditional call to a custom hook (useX inside if)

# Sample output:
# error  React Hook "useState" is called conditionally.
#        React Hooks must be called in the exact same order
#        in every component render.  react-hooks/rules-of-hooks

The linter performs flow-insensitive analysis — it reports potential violations based on code structure rather than runtime values. This means it may flag conditional checks that cannot actually change at runtime (like
only when you are certain the condition is compile-time constant and the hook call count can never change.
Fiber Stability and When the List Resets

A component's fiber node is reused across re-renders as long as the component stays mounted and its position in the tree does not change. The same linked list is walked on every re-render. Only three events break this continuity and reset state: the component unmounts, the component's
prop changes, or the component type at that tree position changes (e.g., swapping
in the same slot). All other changes — props, parent re-renders, context updates — preserve the linked list.
State identity survives re-renders and prop changes. It resets only on unmount, key change, or component type swap.

Hook call order is the only mechanism React uses to associate hook state with a specific call site. Each component instance carries a singly linked list of hook objects on its fiber node. The cursor advances by one per hook call, matching calls to slots. A conditional skip shifts all subsequent mappings, reading wrong data or crashing. Keys manipulate fiber identity, resetting state identity entirely. Custom hooks expand into the same list, so all calls inside them must also be unconditional and in a stable order.

content: : React uses a linked list of hook objects on each fiber, walked in order by a cursor on every render
: first render appends objects; re-renders advance the cursor through the same list
: a conditional hook skip causes every following hook to read the wrong stored value
: state identity is tied to a fiber instance; unmounting destroys the list and resets all state on remount
forces a new fiber, discarding the old list
: their internal hook calls occupy consecutive slots in the caller's list; conditional calls violate rule 1 for all their slots
: speculative renders walk the same list repeatedly; stable order is critical for correctness when a render is abandoned and restarted
